votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the helper functions and the specification logic, matching the Coq definitions for all concrete inputs.
The Python code correctly implements the helper functions and the specification logic, matching the mutually exclusive conditions of the Coq specification.

The Python code correctly implements the logic of the Coq specification, handling the empty list, lists containing zero, and other lists with the same conditions and calculations for sum_abs and prod_sign.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. signZ matches Z.compare behavior, sum_abs and prod_sign match their Coq recursive definitions. The spec function prod_signs_spec correctly handles all three disjunctive cases: empty list returns None check, list containing 0 returns Some 0 check, and non-empty list without 0 returns Some(sum_abs * prod_sign) check. The Python uses 'res is None' for the None case and 'res is not None and res == value' for Some cases, which correctly models the Coq option type behavior on concrete inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. signZ, sum_abs, and prod_sign match their Coq counterparts. The prod_signs_spec function checks the same three cases: empty list returns None, list containing 0 returns Some 0, and non-empty list without 0 returns Some(sum_abs * prod_sign). The Python logic correctly captures these disjunctive conditions and will return the same boolean judgment for any concrete (arr, res) pair.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. signZ, sum_abs, and prod_sign match their Coq counterparts. The prod_signs_spec function checks the same three cases: (1) empty list returns None, (2) list containing 0 returns Some 0, (3) non-empty list without 0 returns Some(sum_abs * prod_sign). The Python logic correctly captures these disjunctive conditions and will return the same boolean judgment for any concrete (arr, res) pair.
Python mirrors the Coq cases: None for empty list, 0 if any zero is present, otherwise Some(sum_abs*prod_sign). Helper functions signZ, sum_abs, and prod_sign match Coq definitions on integers.

Branches match the Coq spec: [] -> None; contains 0 -> 0; otherwise Some(sum_abs(arr)*prod_sign(arr)). Helper functions signZ, sum_abs, and prod_sign are faithful, using None/value to represent option.
Helper functions mirror Coq (signZ, sum_abs, prod_sign), and the spec cases match exactly: empty -> None, contains 0 -> 0, otherwise Some(sum_abs(arr) * prod_sign(arr)).
